title: Mark 2
subtitle: Healing and Controversy: Authority Over Sin, Sabbath, and Society

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Baptism of Jesus

sections.header: Verses 1–12 — The Paralysed Man Lowered Through the Roof

heb.paragraph: Your sins are forgiven — aphiēntai. The same root used in the Lord’s Prayer and throughout the NT. The paralytic’s physical condition is the visible manifestation of the deeper problem Jesus addresses first. The healing proves the forgiveness is real.

places.name: Capernaum — Jesus’s house
places.coords: Northwest shore of the Sea of Galilee; “his own town” (2:1)
places.text: Capernaum is Jesus’s operational base. The crowd that fills the house and spills outside reflects his rapid notoriety across Galilee (1:28). The roof-opening through which the paralytic is lowered would have been a light brushwood-and-plaster construction typical of Galilean domestic architecture.

refs.note: The Son of Man received authority, glory and sovereign power — Jesus’s claim to forgive sins invokes this Danielic title.
I, even I, am he who blots out your transgressions — YHWH’s exclusive claim, which is why the scribes recognize the implication of Jesus’s words.
He forgives all your sins and heals all your diseases — the psalm that links forgiveness and physical restoration, the precise combination Jesus performs.

mar.source: Joel Marcus, Mark 1–8 / 8–16, Anchor Bible (2000/2009) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Marcus: The roof-opening detail is significant historically. Galilean village houses typically had flat roofs of wooden beams, reeds, and packed mud — easily removable for lowering a pallet. This is not a tile roof (as Luke's version has it). Mark's version may reflect the Palestinian setting more accurately. The four friends' action creates a social disturbance that Jesus converts into a theological teaching moment.

Marcus: "The Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive sins" — this is the first Son of Man saying in Mark. The title (bar enasha in Aramaic) was an ordinary idiom for "a person" or "I," but it also carried the exalted Danielic resonance of 7:13-14. Marcus argues that Mark's Jesus exploits the ambiguity deliberately: the title asserts both the humanity of the one who forgives and his divine-Danielic authority.

rho.source: David Rhoads & Donald Michie, Mark as Story (3rd ed., 2012) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Rhoads: The healing of the paralytic introduces a narrative technique that will recur throughout Mark 2-3: the embedded controversy. The crowd waits outside; friends create the opening from above; Jesus addresses the paralytic; the scribes object silently; Jesus reads their thoughts. The story moves through five distinct perspectives and shifts of attention in twelve verses — a narrative density characteristic of Mark's storytelling economy.

Rhoads: "But I want you to know..." — Jesus directly addresses the narrator's audience as much as the scribes. This breaking of the story-world frame (addressing the narrative challenge as though answering objectors outside the scene) is rare in Mark but significant. The healing becomes theatrical proof, performed for the watching crowd and the skeptical scribes simultaneously.

cat.source: Thomas Aquinas (compiler), Catena Aurea — Patristic Commentary, Public Domain

notes.note: Chrysostom observes that the four men who lowered the paralytic through the roof display a faith notable for its ingenuity — they did not give up when the crowd blocked the door, but found another way. This, says Aquinas following Ambrose, is the figure of intercessory prayer: the faith of others can carry the helpless to Christ. The scribes' objection "Who can forgive sins but God alone?" is, for Cyril of Alexandria, the most accurate theological statement in the chapter — which is precisely why Jesus accepts the premise and fulfils it.

The question "Which is easier?" is, for Theophylact, a rhetorical trap Jesus sets for the scribes: healing is visible and testable; forgiveness is not. By performing the visible sign, Jesus provides evidence for the invisible authority. Chrysostom notes that the crowd glorified God, not Jesus — and Mark records this without correction, which is itself instructive. The miracle does not compel worship but directs it; the proper response to divine power is theological reorientation, not merely amazement.
When Jesus saw their faith — the faith of the four friends who carried the paralytic. Corporate faith as the context for individual healing. Jesus responds to the community’s determination.

Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive sins — the first appearance of this title. Jesus uses it as a self-designation that is simultaneously humble (human figure) and exalted (Danielic universal ruler).

Calvin: The scribes are technically correct that only God forgives sins. Their error is not in the theology but in the conclusion — they should have asked who this man is, not accused him of blasphemy. Jesus’s response does not deny their theological principle; it confirms it while claiming to embody it.
The healing is a “perceptible sign” that authenticates the imperceptible grace. God accommodates human weakness by providing visible evidence of invisible realities.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: Son of Man — ho huios tou anthrōpou. This phrase occurs 14 times in Mark. It is Jesus’s preferred self-designation — simultaneously evoking Dan 7:13 (heavenly authority) and the human solidarity of Ps 8:4 (son of man as mortal). The ambiguity is deliberate.
In full view of them all — emprosthen pantōn. The public nature of the healing is emphasized. This is not private certification but community witness.

hist.context: The healing of the paralytic through the roof is the first direct controversy with the scribes and the first explicit claim of divine authority over sin. Jesus’s question “Which is easier” is a logical trap: the visible miracle proves the invisible pardon. The Son of Man title (v.10) appears for the first time — the Danielic figure who has authority on earth.

sections.header: Verses 13–22 — Levi Called; Eating with Sinners; Fasting Question

heb.paragraph: Fasting in Second Temple Judaism marked mourning, penitence, and waiting. Jesus’s answer reframes the question: while the bridegroom is present there is nothing to mourn. The disciples’ non-fasting is not irreverence but recognition that the messianic era has arrived.

places.name: Capernaum — Jesus’s house
places.coords: Northwest shore of the Sea of Galilee; “his own town” (2:1)
places.text: Capernaum is Jesus’s operational base. The crowd that fills the house and spills outside reflects his rapid notoriety across Galilee (1:28). The roof-opening through which the paralytic is lowered would have been a light brushwood-and-plaster construction typical of Galilean domestic architecture.

refs.note: David eating the consecrated bread — the precedent Jesus cites for his disciples’ grain-picking. Human need overrides ritual restriction when the anointed king’s need is at stake.
I desire mercy, not sacrifice — the OT principle Jesus embodies. Table fellowship with sinners is mercy over ritual purity.
As a bridegroom rejoices over his bride — the divine-human marriage metaphor Jesus applies to himself. His presence is the wedding, not the time for fasting.

mar.source: Joel Marcus, Mark 1–8 / 8–16, Anchor Bible (2000/2009) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Marcus: The call of Levi at the toll booth is structurally parallel to the fishermen's call (1:16-20): Jesus sees, calls, and the man follows immediately. But the setting is more politically charged. Tax collectors (telōnai) collected indirect taxes at customs posts and were widely despised as economic collaborators with Rome. In rabbinic literature they are regularly grouped with robbers and murderers. The call of Levi across this social-religious barrier signals that the kingdom's boundaries are being redrawn.

Marcus: "The Sabbath was made for man, not man for the Sabbath" has a partial parallel in the later Mekhilta de-Rabbi Yishmael (on Exod 31:14): "The Sabbath is given to you, not you to the Sabbath." This rabbinic principle, which Jesus' saying anticipates, shows that the statement is not anti-Jewish but represents a valid position within Jewish debate. The radicality is the second clause: "So the Son of Man is Lord of the Sabbath" — a claim with no rabbinic parallel.

rho.source: David Rhoads & Donald Michie, Mark as Story (3rd ed., 2012) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Rhoads: The meal at Levi's house introduces the "many tax collectors and sinners" as a social type — background characters who function as evidence of Jesus's mission scope. The Pharisees are characterized by their question to the disciples (not directly to Jesus), which places them as observers unable to engage the main character directly. Jesus's responses in 2:17-22 are three self-contained aphorisms rather than a sustained argument — the character communicates through compressed wisdom, not extended discourse.

Rhoads: The grain-field Sabbath controversy is the second of five controversies in Mark 2-3. The narrator characterizes the Pharisees primarily through their questions and accusations, never giving them interiority. The effect is to present them as functionally antagonistic without explaining their motives — a rhetorical choice that shapes the reader's sympathy. Jesus consistently outmaneuvers them through scriptural counter-argument, establishing his rhetorical superiority within their own tradition.

cat.source: Thomas Aquinas (compiler), Catena Aurea — Patristic Commentary, Public Domain

notes.note: The calling of Levi at the tax booth is, for Chrysostom, a deliberate provocation: Jesus calls the most socially ostracized professional imaginable, and then reclines at table with his colleagues. Bede notes that the meal at Levi's house is the first extended fellowship scene in Mark — Jesus eating with sinners before any of them have repented, which is itself a parable of grace. The saying "I have not come to call the righteous, but sinners" is, for Augustine, ironic: the self-declared righteous exclude themselves; the declaration of need is already the beginning of coming.

The fasting controversy and the Sabbath dispute are, for Cyril of Alexandria, two faces of one argument: the old forms of religion are vessels, not the wine. The new wine of the kingdom cannot be contained in the wineskins of legal observance designed for the preparatory era. The Sabbath saying "The Sabbath was made for man, not man for the Sabbath" is, for Chrysostom, not an abolition but a re-grounding: the purpose of rest is human flourishing, not ritual compliance. The Son of Man's lordship over the Sabbath is the authoritative reinterpretation that only the lawgiver can provide.

Levi the tax collector — a collaborator with Roman occupation. His call is the most socially scandalous of the four: he is not merely a fisherman but a traitor to his own people. That Jesus calls him demonstrates the unconditioned nature of grace.

The Sabbath was made for man, not man for the Sabbath — a principle that has no parallel in rabbinic literature of the period. Then the climax: the Son of Man is Lord of the Sabbath. Jesus does not merely interpret the Sabbath; he claims ownership of it.

Calvin: The new patch and new wine parables are warnings to the disciples as much as rebukes to the Pharisees. The gospel cannot be domesticated into old religious structures. It requires new forms, new practices, new community. Calvin uses this passage against those who would pour the Reformation’s wine into medieval ecclesiastical wineskins.
The Sabbath was made for human flourishing. Calvin insists that Jesus is not abolishing the Sabbath but restoring its original purpose — which the Pharisees had obscured with regulatory accumulation.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: Levi son of Alphaeus — identified in the parallel accounts as Matthew (Matt 9:9). He was a telōnēs (tax collector/toll collector) — a position that required collaboration with Roman authority and was associated with extortion. His call is the most socially transgressive in Mark.

In the days of Abiathar the high priest — a textual problem: 1 Sam 21 says Ahimelech, Abiathar’s father, was the priest. Various solutions have been proposed: Abiathar was present but not officiating; the title refers to the Abiathar era rather than the moment; or it is a genuine error in the tradition.

hist.context: Mark 2:13-28 groups three controversy stories that reveal Jesus’s radical claim to re-order the religious life of Israel: eating with sinners (the physician metaphor), non-fasting (the bridegroom/new wine metaphor), and grain-picking on the Sabbath (David precedent + the Son of Man as Sabbath Lord). Each controversy escalates the implicit Christological claim.

sections.header: Verses 23–28 — Lord of the Sabbath: The Son of Man

heb.paragraph: Your sins are forgiven — aphiēntai. The same root used in the Lord’s Prayer and throughout the NT. The paralytic’s physical condition is the visible manifestation of the deeper problem Jesus addresses first. The healing proves the forgiveness is real.

places.name: Capernaum — Jesus’s house
places.coords: Northwest shore of the Sea of Galilee; “his own town” (2:1)
places.text: Capernaum is Jesus’s operational base. The crowd that fills the house and spills outside reflects his rapid notoriety across Galilee (1:28). The roof-opening through which the paralytic is lowered would have been a light brushwood-and-plaster construction typical of Galilean domestic architecture.

refs.note: The Son of Man received authority, glory and sovereign power — Jesus’s claim to forgive sins invokes this Danielic title.
I, even I, am he who blots out your transgressions — YHWH’s exclusive claim, which is why the scribes recognize the implication of Jesus’s words.
He forgives all your sins and heals all your diseases — the psalm that links forgiveness and physical restoration, the precise combination Jesus performs.

mar.source: Joel Marcus, Mark 1–8 / 8–16, Anchor Bible (2000/2009) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Marcus: On 28 — Lord of the Sabbath: The Son of Man: omposition" well attested in Second Temple Jewish midrash. The Qumran community (1QS 8:12-14) also applied Isa 40:3 to their own wilderness mission, suggesting that this text was a live messianic-preparatory image in Jesus's era.

rho.source: David Rhoads & Donald Michie, Mark as Story (3rd ed., 2012) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Rhoads: On 28 — Lord of the Sabbath: The Son of Man: iah, the Son of God." The narrator tells the reader what no character in the story yet knows. This creates a sustained dramatic irony — the audience watches characters struggle with a question (who is Jesus?) whose answer has been given in verse 1.

cat.source: Thomas Aquinas (compiler), Catena Aurea — Patristic Commentary, Public Domain

notes.note: Patristic commentary on 28 — Lord of the Sabbath: The Son of Man:  action. The quotation from Isaiah and Malachi (vv.2-3) in one breath is deliberate: Aquinas, following Bede, says it shows the two offices of the Forerunner — as the messenger of the covenant (Malachi) and as the voice in the wilderness (Isaiah). The prophecy precedes the person; the word precedes the Word.

Calvin: On 28 — Lord of the Sabbath: The Son of Man: ng — because the gospel is primarily the announcement of salvation. The prophetic testimony grounds it in ancient covenant fulfilment, not new invention. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET Note: On 28 — Lord of the Sabbath: The Son of Man: ingle event at the end. The wild animals are unique to Mark — possibly Edenic restoration imagery or Markan geographical realism about the desert terrain. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

hist.context: The healing of the paralytic through the roof is the first direct controversy with the scribes and the first explicit claim of divine authority over sin. Jesus’s question “Which is easier” is a logical trap: the visible miracle proves the invisible pardon. The Son of Man title (v.10) appears for the first time — the Danielic figure who has authority on earth.

ppl.name: Levi (Matthew)
ppl.text: Called at his toll booth — the most socially transgressive call in Mark. His banquet with tax collectors and sinners triggers the first dining controversy.

ppl.name: The Scribes/Pharisees
ppl.role: Teachers of the law; first opponents
ppl.text: They appear first in 2:6 (thinking in their hearts) and are the primary human antagonists throughout the Galilean ministry.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVThe Sabbath was made for man, not man for the Sabbath. So the Son of Man is Lord even of the Sabbath.ESVThe Sabbath was made for man, not man for the Sabbath. So the Son of Man is lord even of the Sabbath.KJVThe sabbath was made for man, and not man for the sabbath: Therefore the Son of man is Lord also of the sabbath.NASBThe Sabbath was made for man, and not man for the Sabbath. So the Son of Man is Lord even of the Sabbath.NLTThe Sabbath was made to meet the needs of people, and not people to meet the requirements of the Sabbath. So the Son of Man is Lord, even over the Sabbath!

src.title: Tosefta Shabbat
src.quote: Rabbinic text stating that the Sabbath was given to Israel and not Israel to the Sabbath — a principle similar to Jesus’s v.27 statement.
src.note: Jesus’s Sabbath teaching draws on existing rabbinic logic but radicalizes it by adding the Son of Man claim.

src.title: Josephus, Antiquities 18.17
src.quote: Josephus confirms that tax collectors were widely despised in first-century Jewish society as collaborators and extortionists.
src.note: Confirms the social scandal of Jesus’s association with tax collectors in Mark 2:15-16.

rec.text: Augustine used the physician metaphor (2:17) as the defining statement of Jesus’s mission: he came for the sick, not the well. The church is not a society of the perfect but a hospital for sinners.
Bonhoeffer used the calling of Levi (2:14) to argue that discipleship is not the result of extended deliberation but immediate obedience to a direct call. The decision is made before it is reasoned.

rows.label: The Bridegroom Metaphor — First Passion Shadow

hebtext.note: Your sins are forgiven — aphiēntai. The same root used in the Lord’s Prayer and throughout the NT. The paralytic’s physical condition is the visible manifestation of the deeper problem Jesus addresses first. The healing proves the forgiveness is real.

Fasting in Second Temple Judaism marked mourning, penitence, and waiting. Jesus’s answer reframes the question: while the bridegroom is present there is nothing to mourn. The disciples’ non-fasting is not irreverence but recognition that the messianic era has arrived.

Your sins are forgiven — aphiēntai. The same root used in the Lord’s Prayer and throughout the NT. The paralytic’s physical condition is the visible manifestation of the deeper problem Jesus addresses first. The healing proves the forgiveness is real.

tx.title: Returning to Capernaum — "at home"
tx.content: Some MSS readeis oikon(into a house); others readen oikō(at home/in the house). NA28 prefersen oikō.
tx.note: The reading affects whether Jesus is in someone's house or his own established residence; both fit the Capernaum-base theme.

tx.title: Pharisees addressing the disciples
tx.content: NA28/UBS5"the scribes of the Pharisees" (hoi grammateis tōn Pharisaiōn); variant: "the scribes and the Pharisees."
tx.note: "Scribes of the Pharisees" is the more specific reading and likely original; the simpler "scribes and Pharisees" is a harmonisation.

positions.name: Abolition view
positions.proponents: Some Reformation and modern interpreters read 2:27-28 as annulling the Sabbath command entirely for Christians.

positions.name: Transformation view
positions.proponents: Jesus restores the Sabbath’s original purpose rather than abolishing it. The Son of Man as Sabbath Lord re-establishes the Sabbath under new covenant terms.

themes.note: Mark 2 is the first major conflict chapter: every pericope ends with a Christological claim that escalates the implicit question of who Jesus is.

coaching.tip: 'Who can forgive sins but God alone?' (v. 7). The scribes reason correctly; they draw the wrong conclusion. Jesus proves authority to forgive by healing—the visible validates the invisible.

'The Sabbath was made for man, not man for the Sabbath' (v. 27). Jesus doesn't abolish Sabbath but recalibrates it: the institution serves humanity, not vice versa. The Son of Man is Lord even of this.

prayer_prompt: As four friends tear open a roof to bring the paralysed man to Jesus, consider asking God who in your life needs you to carry them into His presence — and whether anything is stopping you.
